sessions.title: Agents in the Real World
sessions.set_the_stage:
Open with the practitioner's view. Before a single line of code, we answer the questions a working professional actually asks: what is an agent, where does it help, where does it fall down, and how do we keep it safe.

Walk the room through the slides. They are built to land one idea at a time, with a wrong way and a right way for each. Pause on the risks and the human-in-the-loop slide; that is the grown-up part of the conversation.

By the end, the room should be able to say in one sentence what an agent reads, decides, and produces, and name the one place it could quietly go wrong.

sessions.title: The Humanics of AI
sessions.set_the_stage:
This is the thinking layer. The best agent-builders are not the fastest typists; they are the clearest thinkers. We teach three habits: first principles, systems thinking, and problem framing.

The goal is not abstract. Every habit points straight at the recruitment build coming next. Breaking a task into atoms, seeing the whole pipe, and framing a sharp question are exactly what makes the agent easy to design.

Land it: think clearly first, and the code writes itself.

sessions.title: The Mechanics of AI
sessions.set_the_stage:
Now the building blocks, kept ruthlessly simple: a model (text in, text out), a prompt (your instructions), structured output (neat data, not prose), and a key (just a password).

Then the anatomy every agent shares, input to brain to output, and the move that makes real agents work: decomposition into three single-purpose agents that hand clean data down the line.

This module is the bridge from idea to build. Keep it tight and concrete; resist the urge to explain tokens or transformers.

build.title: Hand out the pack
build.description: Give each student the 25-resume PDF and the one-page job description. No laptops open yet.

build.title: Rank by hand, timed
build.description: Pick the top 5 to interview, one line of reasoning each. Start a visible 15-minute timer. Write down your own time.

build.title: Surface the steps
build.description: Ask three students how they did it. Draw the shared steps on the board: read, pull the key facts, compare to the JD, rank, recommend. These become the agent design.

sessions.title: The Recruitment Challenge
sessions.set_the_stage:
The problem: here are 25 resumes and one job description. Recommend the top 5 to interview. Every student does it by hand first, no AI, and times themselves.

This is not busywork. The by-hand attempt creates two things we need later: a real 'before' number to measure the agent against, and a felt understanding of the actual steps, read, pull facts, compare to the JD, rank, hand over.

Most will not finish all 25 in the time. That is the point. Let the room feel the load the agent is about to lift.

build.title: Agent 1, Ingest and Extract
build.description: Takes the resume files and returns clean, structured facts for each candidate: name, years, skills, tools, evidence of impact. This is the contract the next agent depends on.

build.title: Agent 2, Judge and Prioritise
build.description: Takes those facts, scores them against the JD rubric with evidence for each score, applies any hard rules in code, and returns a ranked top 5.

build.title: Agent 3, Communicate
build.description: Takes the top 5 and drafts a clear recommendation to the hiring manager. It drafts; the human reads and sends. Never auto-send.

build.title: Draw the contract
build.description: Make the handshake explicit: Agent 1's output shape is exactly Agent 2's input shape. Fix the fields once and the pipeline holds.

sessions.title: Design the Agents (No Code)
sessions.set_the_stage:
Turn the board into a design, still no code. First state the pure logic: ingest the resumes, extract the key information, build a rubric from the JD, compare, prioritise the top 5, and communicate them to the hiring manager.

Then map that logic onto the agent anatomy and split it into three single-purpose agents. Students should leave this session able to draw the whole solution on paper before they ever touch Colab.

Hold the line on the human gate: Agent 3 drafts the note; a person sends it.

build.title: Pre-flight
build.description: Run the setup cells top to bottom: install, paste key, engine, load the 25 resumes and the JD. Look for the green confirmations.

build.title: Run Agent 1
build.description: Extract structured facts from all 25 resumes. Watch messy documents become a clean table.

build.title: Run Agent 2
build.description: Score against the rubric with evidence, apply the hard rule, and reveal the ranked top 5.

build.title: Run Agent 3
build.description: Draft the recommendation to the hiring manager. Read it aloud. Do not send: that is the human's call.

build.title: Validate
build.description: Lay the agent's 5 next to the by-hand 5. Discuss the overlap and the differences. This is how you learn to trust an agent.

sessions.title: Build It Live
sessions.set_the_stage:
Now the facilitator builds the physical implementation live, in a Google Colab notebook, running it step by step on the real 25 resumes. This uses the paid demo key so it never stalls in front of the room.

Narrate every cell back to the design on the board: this cell is Agent 1 extracting, this cell is Agent 2 judging, this cell drafts for the human. The code should feel like the obvious transcription of a design they already understand.

Close the loop on validation: compare the agent's top 5 to the room's by-hand top 5 from yesterday. Where they agree, where they differ, and why.

build.title: Pick from the palette
build.description: Choose the use-case closest to your world. Same three agents, different job.

build.title: Change only the marked lines
build.description: Swap the documents, the extract fields, the rubric, and the hard rule. Everything else stays exactly as it is.

build.title: Run on the fallback
build.description: Leave the fallback switch on. It runs on saved results so nothing blocks you. Flip it off only if you want to go live on your own key.

build.title: Defend one choice
build.description: Pair up and justify one design choice to another pair: why that criterion, why that weight, why that hard rule. Building is shallow; defending is deep.

sessions.title: Build Your Own Agent
sessions.set_the_stage:
Hand the notebook to the students. They pick one use-case from the palette, from finops, support, sales, and more, and configure the exact same three-agent design for it. Only the marked lines change.

This run defaults to the fallback switch, so it works with zero friction even if the free tier hiccups. Name that out loud: you already learned the live key this morning; this run is about the pattern, not the plumbing. The live path is one flip away for anyone who wants it.

The point is ownership. When a finance student watches the same machine triage invoices, or a support student watches it sort tickets, the pattern lands in a world they care about.

build.title: Tell the story
build.description: Each student writes their one-line outcome: the by-hand time, the agent time, and the job it does. That is the interview answer.

build.title: Find three piles
build.description: List three piles of similar documents in your world that this same design could sort. That is where your next agent lives.

sessions.title: The Valuenomics of AI
sessions.set_the_stage:
Close with value. A working agent is not the prize; the outcome is. We trace the chain from data to insight to narrative to influence to outcome, and show how the same shape unlocks value across domains.

Then widen the toolbox: beyond screen-and-rank, the patterns of extract-and-summarise, monitor-and-alert, draft-and-refine, and answer-over-your-docs, so students leave seeing agents everywhere.

End on the honest, defensible story each student can tell a recruiter: a real agent, built for their world, measured against their own by-hand baseline.

ideas.name: Recruitment Screener
ideas.for_whom: You, if HR or campus placements is your world
ideas.problem: A recruiter drowns in 200 resumes per role and skims the first 20 by gut feel.
ideas.what_app_does: Extracts facts from every resume, scores them against the JD rubric with evidence, and returns a ranked shortlist to interview.

ideas.name: Invoice Triage
ideas.for_whom: You, if finance or FinOps is your world
ideas.problem: Accounts payable processes hundreds of invoices; the risky ones hide in the pile.
ideas.what_app_does: Extracts each invoice's key fields, flags anything needing human approval by a hard rule, and prioritises the rest for payment.

ideas.name: Support Ticket Triage
ideas.for_whom: You, if customer support is your world
ideas.problem: A support queue mixes trivial and urgent; the loudest ticket is rarely the most important.
ideas.what_app_does: Reads each ticket, scores impact and urgency with evidence, and auto-escalates anything touching money or legal.

ideas.name: Lead Scorer
ideas.for_whom: You, if sales or growth is your world
ideas.problem: Reps chase every inbound lead equally and waste the week on poor-fit ones.
ideas.what_app_does: Extracts each lead's signals, scores fit against your ideal-customer rubric, and ranks who to call first.

ideas.name: Expense Claim Checker
ideas.for_whom: You, if finance or audit is your world
ideas.problem: Expense claims are rubber-stamped or randomly sampled; policy breaches slip through.
ideas.what_app_does: Extracts each claim's details, flags policy violations by hard rule, and ranks the rest for a quick human review.

ideas.name: Incident Report Triage
ideas.for_whom: You, if operations or safety is your world
ideas.problem: Incident reports pile up and the serious ones are not read first.
ideas.what_app_does: Reads each report, scores severity with evidence, and pushes the critical ones to the top with a reason.

ideas.name: Application Screener
ideas.for_whom: You, if admissions or programs is your world
ideas.problem: A program gets far more applications than it can read carefully.
ideas.what_app_does: Extracts each application's facts, scores them against the selection rubric with evidence, and returns a shortlist.

ideas.name: Feedback Prioritiser
ideas.for_whom: You, if product or CX is your world
ideas.problem: Hundreds of reviews and survey verbatims arrive; the churn-risk signals are buried.
ideas.what_app_does: Reads each piece of feedback, scores it by churn risk and theme, and ranks what to act on this week.
